title: Active transport — moving things against the flow
examTip: Active transport is a guaranteed AQA topic. You need to know it moves substances AGAINST the concentration gradient, it REQUIRES energy from respiration, and you must give real examples — root hair cells and the gut. Saying 'against the gradient' without adding 'using energy' loses marks.
introTips: Diffusion and osmosis move substances the easy way — downhill, from high to low concentration. Active transport is different: it moves substances uphill, and that costs energy.
AQA wants you to explain WHY active transport is necessary — sometimes the body needs a substance even when it's already more concentrated inside the cell.
You'll also compare all three transport mechanisms — diffusion, osmosis, and active transport — which is a common exam question.
intro: Your cells are picky. Sometimes they need a substance — say, a mineral ion or a glucose molecule — even when there's already more of it inside the cell than outside. Diffusion won't work in that direction; you'd need energy to push it 'uphill'. That's exactly what active transport does, and it's one of the most important processes in your body.

keyPoints.heading: What active transport is
keyPoints.content: Active transport is the movement of substances from an area of low concentration to an area of high concentration — that is, against the concentration gradient. Because this is moving 'uphill', it requires energy in the form of ATP, which comes from respiration. This is what makes it fundamentally different from diffusion and osmosis, which both move substances down the gradient and need no energy input.
writeBullets: Active transport: moves substances from LOW → HIGH concentration
Direction is AGAINST the concentration gradient
Unlike diffusion and osmosis — those are passive (no energy needed)

keyTerms.def: Movement of substances from a region of low concentration to high concentration, against the concentration gradient, using energy from respiration
The difference in concentration of a substance between two areas; diffusion moves down it, active transport moves against it
Adenosine triphosphate — the molecule that stores and releases energy in cells, produced by respiration

keyPoints.memTip: Think of active transport like cycling uphill — it goes the WRONG way (against the gradient) and you need ENERGY to do it. If a question says 'against the concentration gradient', active transport is always the answer. Passive = no energy. Active = energy needed. That distinction is worth marks every time.

keyPoints.heading: How it works — carrier proteins
keyPoints.content: Active transport uses carrier proteins embedded in the cell membrane. The substance to be transported binds to the carrier protein on one side of the membrane. The protein then uses energy from respiration (ATP) to change shape and release the substance on the other side — the high-concentration side. The carrier protein acts like a revolving door that only goes one way, powered by the cell's energy supply.
writeBullets: Carrier proteins are embedded in the cell membrane
Substance binds to carrier protein on the low-concentration side
Energy (ATP from respiration) powers a shape change in the protein
Substance is released on the high-concentration side
Each carrier protein is specific — it only transports certain molecules

keyTerms.def: A protein in the cell membrane that binds to specific molecules and uses energy to transport them across the membrane
The selectively permeable barrier surrounding the cell that controls what enters and leaves

keyPoints.memTip: Carrier proteins are the 'engines' of active transport — they do the physical work of moving substances across the membrane. AQA sometimes asks you to 'describe how active transport works': always mention (1) carrier protein, (2) energy from respiration, (3) against the concentration gradient. Three points, three marks.

keyPoints.heading: Where it happens in the body
keyPoints.content: Two key examples you must know. First, root hair cells in plant roots use active transport to absorb mineral ions (e.g. nitrates) from the soil — even when the soil has a lower mineral concentration than the root cell. Second, the small intestine uses active transport to absorb glucose into the blood after a meal — even when the blood already contains more glucose than the gut contents. In both cases, the cell needs the substance regardless of the gradient, so it has to spend energy to get it.
writeBullets: Root hair cells: absorb mineral ions (e.g. nitrates) from soil water via active transport
Soil mineral concentration is LOWER than inside the root cell — so active transport is needed
Small intestine: absorbs glucose from gut contents into the blood
Gut glucose concentration can be LOWER than blood — so diffusion is impossible, active transport is used
Both examples: substance needed by the organism even when gradient works against it

keyTerms.def: Specialised plant cell with a large surface area that absorbs water and mineral ions from the soil
Inorganic ions such as nitrates, absorbed by plants and essential for growth

keyPoints.memTip: Two examples, same logic: the body NEEDS the substance even when it's already more concentrated inside. Root hair cells + minerals from soil. Small intestine + glucose from gut. Learn both — AQA often asks for a named example. 'Root hair cell absorbs nitrate ions against the concentration gradient using energy from respiration' is a model answer worth memorising.

keyPoints.heading: Diffusion, osmosis, active transport — compared
keyPoints.content: All three processes move substances across membranes, but in different ways. Diffusion: moves any small molecule (gases, dissolved substances) from high to low concentration — no energy needed, goes down the gradient. Osmosis: moves water only through a partially permeable membrane from dilute solution to concentrated solution (down the water potential gradient) — no energy needed. Active transport: moves specific substances from low to high concentration — energy required, goes against the gradient. The key examiner distinction: if it needs energy, it's active transport.
writeBullets: Diffusion: any small molecule, high → low, no energy, passive
Osmosis: water only, dilute → concentrated, no energy, passive, needs partially permeable membrane
Active transport: specific molecules, low → high, uses energy from respiration, active
All three involve movement across a cell membrane
Examiner key: energy involved? → active transport. Water only? → osmosis. Everything else passive → diffusion.

keyTerms.def: Net movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration, requiring no energy
Net movement of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane from a dilute solution to a more concentrated solution
A membrane that allows water molecules to pass through but not solute molecules

keyPoints.memTip: When a comparison question comes up, build the table in your head: Direction — Diffusion (down), Osmosis (down, water only), Active (against). Energy — Diffusion (no), Osmosis (no), Active (yes). What moves — Diffusion (any small molecule), Osmosis (water), Active (specific substances). Three rows, three columns. That's the whole answer.

tables.title: Diffusion vs Osmosis vs Active Transport
rows: Rate of respiration, number of carrier proteins

1.question: Which of the following best describes active transport?
options: Movement of water from dilute to concentrated solution with no energy
Movement of substances from high to low concentration with no energy
Movement of substances from low to high concentration using energy from respiration
Movement of gases down a concentration gradient through a membrane
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right — against the gradient, using energy from respiration. That's the definition AQA wants.
1.wrongFeedback: Active transport moves substances AGAINST the concentration gradient — from low to high. That goes uphill, so it needs energy from respiration. Diffusion and osmosis are passive and go downhill.

3.question: Why does the small intestine need active transport to absorb glucose, rather than just relying on diffusion?
options: Glucose molecules are too large to diffuse
The concentration of glucose in the blood can be higher than in the gut, so diffusion would go the wrong way
The small intestine does not have enough surface area for diffusion
Glucose is a liquid and cannot diffuse
3.correctFeedback: Spot on. When blood glucose is already high, diffusion would move glucose back out of the blood into the gut — the opposite of what you want. Active transport forces it in regardless.
3.wrongFeedback: The issue is the gradient. When blood glucose concentration is higher than in the gut, diffusion would push glucose the wrong way. Active transport uses energy to move glucose into the blood regardless of the gradient — that's why it's needed.

commonMistakes: Saying active transport 'doesn't need energy' — it always needs energy from respiration. That's the whole point. Passive transport (diffusion, osmosis) is the one that needs no energy.
Describing active transport as moving from high to low concentration — it's the opposite. It goes AGAINST the gradient: low → high. If you write the wrong direction, you lose the mark.
Forgetting to link energy to respiration — 'uses energy' alone isn't enough for AQA. The full phrase is 'uses energy from respiration' or 'uses ATP produced by respiration'.
Mixing up osmosis and active transport when water is involved — osmosis is always passive and only moves water. Active transport never moves water; it moves ions and molecules like glucose.
Not giving a named example when the question asks for one — 'cells in the body' won't score. Always say 'root hair cells absorbing mineral ions' or 'small intestine absorbing glucose'.
examTips: If a question asks you to 'explain why active transport is needed', the answer structure is always: (1) state the concentrations (what's higher where), (2) explain that diffusion would go the wrong way, (3) say active transport moves it against the gradient using energy from respiration.

AQA loves asking 'what would happen to active transport if respiration stopped?' — the answer is that active transport would stop too, because there's no energy (ATP) available. Cells that are poisoned or run out of oxygen lose active transport first.
In 6-mark questions comparing transport mechanisms, always cover all three (diffusion, osmosis, active transport) and for each one state: direction, energy requirement, and what substance moves.

videoLinks.label: Active Transport Revision

revisionCardBullets: Active transport: LOW → HIGH concentration (against the gradient)
Needs energy from RESPIRATION (ATP) — this is what makes it 'active'
Uses carrier proteins in the cell membrane to move substances across
Example 1: root hair cells absorb mineral ions (nitrates) from soil — soil concentration lower than inside root
Example 2: small intestine absorbs glucose into blood — even when blood glucose is already higher
Diffusion: passive, high → low, any small molecule
Osmosis: passive, water only, dilute → concentrated, through partially permeable membrane
Active transport: active (energy needed), low → high, specific molecules
If respiration stops → active transport stops (no ATP available)
